We now continue module one of the Make-Peace Business System, Clayton's interview with Tony Flores. Okay, well another concern that I have is that let's say that you do go through this process you are able to convince a client you have that first date you're able to convince them that to take on a bigger you know broader relationship and the last thing that you really wanted to hope that I think the biggest fear I would have and I think a lot of the other writers that that would initiate this process would have is is you know failing or what if it doesn't work out or you can't produce you know across the board improvement that that you're essentially kind of promising the client when you initiate the discussion and so I wonder how do you know you're ready in terms especially in terms of knowledge and skill to approach somebody with a partnership deal you know what point you really know well you know I think obviously no approach to building a copyrighted business is going to work unless your product is there unless your product works it's just like any other company if your product stinks you're going to have a rough a rough go of it but if you can write good solid sales copy that creates even you know incremental increases in response and average sale for a client you already have the basic tools to begin doing this because the natural progression is you know your your potential partner is thinking of you as a copywriter anyway and all you're really doing is you're asking to say you're saying oh you know I'm doing your name acquisition or your new customer acquisition promos now let me take a look at what you do with the customers after they come on board and so you know that's a copywriting function it's not marketing consultation it's not grand strategy it's not even you know execution and procedures it's it's it's still copy so if you have that you can begin doing that now it's just as easy as what I said with SRC you know I got into that very lucrative new contract with them entirely on the basis of copy later on I built an entire marketing department for him I hired 35 graphic artists this is back in the days when we still had to cut paste type on a on an art board with the rubber cement and to handle the quantity of promotions that we were doing for our house file we needed 35 35 artists plus a bunch of copywriters and marketing people and everything else so I built an entire marketing department for him and got heavily involved with with you know a lot of other aspects of the company so but that came later you know first first you key on what you can do well now and just find additional ways to apply it to that company that that business owners company later on and that alone is enough to make you a hell of a lot of money later on of course you're going to find things as you're working with a customer that needs fixing you know you're going to you're going to figure out that maybe there are other media that you know that would work better maybe maybe he should try out multiple step sales strategy instead of a single step maybe he doesn't have an adequate product price ladder you know you want a ladder or a pyramid where at the very bottom you've got a product that's priced attractively for people who've never heard of you before right that there won't be an obstacle to them becoming customers in the first place and at the other end of the ladder you need a you need products that will allow you to maximize revenues from each existing customer and you need incremental steps along the way so you know maybe you'll identify opportunities there maybe you'll see problems on the staff maybe you'll see lack of infrastructure maybe you'll see new tools or programs that need to be bought maybe you know maybe you'll dabble in strategy by offering offer tests or new kind new ways to bump a new customer on the first purchase or new ways to introduce them to different product lines within that the company has and so you know those things will happen naturally as you work together and you'll find that your role just naturally expand within the company and that's why you know I think what's desperately needed today really is is for copywriters to have to develop a broader view of direct marketing you know if you take the AWI course or any of the other copyrighting courses out there you get great advice great direction on how to write a winning promotion but so many of the copywriters that I deal with it that the new copywriters who come out of those kinds of courses they really have never been taught where the grand strategy for the promotion came from why the company developed this product in the first place why they're not doing soft offers you know instead of just hard offers or why they're not trying continuity why you know why this media you know why direct mail when it's so expensive why not try TV what happens to a customer after I bring him in and all of that kind of stuff so you know there's a great book out there that I recommend it's Bob's it's by Bob Stone and I think it's updated almost every year and it's called proven direct marketing methods or maybe a successful direct marketing I think it's successful direct marketing methods by Bob Stone and Amazon has it and I read an ancient version of it I think it was in Sanskrit back in 1973 or four when I first got into this racket so everything that I learned about copywriting was within the context of the overall strategy and the nuts and bolts that I learned from studying that book and it is a dense read and it will take a long time to really work your way through it feel that you've got a good grip on it but it it's a great way to get started does that answer your question yeah it does although it raises it raises another one you get started things are things are going along now obviously though we're copywriters we sell the best possible outcome but but not every partner every partnership deal works out no you know what you how do you how do you really how do you give yourself a higher chance okay succeeding in terms of that good well okay well a couple of things first of all when you're discussing this with with potential partner but if you're the copywriter and you're going in and you're really wanting to develop this and you're discussing this with a potential partner humility is the best possible way to approach it you know rather than saying boy you just signed this deal and make me your partner and I'm gonna explode your company I'm gonna quadruple your company I'm gonna do this I'm gonna do that do the other thing if you do that and and you come in short in any of the above any of the things that you just promised you look like a fool and a failure and and even even if you produced a positive response but not as big as you would hope for the relationship is then weaker than it then it should be but if instead you say hey well why don't we just try this tell like I did with SRC I didn't tell him I was gonna give him a thousand percent bump and sales I just said I think I could do a lot better than what you're doing now you know and so first of all you know setting the expectation in a proper place because you know the honest truth is your client is intelligent or he wouldn't have a company of his own to start with okay and then and and he knows that the successor failure of any given promotion is dependent on a lot of things it could be you know the economy can change in a heartbeat the market could change in a heartbeat the the theme that's chosen for this particular promotion may not resonate you know there are there are a hundred I mean we've been in situations where a guy took years building up his mail quantities and then in November dropped a huge you know his biggest promotion today put all of his money in the mail and it was a for a financial newsletter product and it was all about how Carter was killing the economy you couldn't trust Washington to protect the value of your money and of course that very month Reagan was elected in all of our prospects figured we'd elected Messiah and they didn't need advice anymore because the stock market was gonna go straight up and so my friend just absolutely got killed in the mail so you know there are all kinds of variables and so you lack credibility when you make promises of doubling or quadrupling or quintupling his file and he knows that that's just that's unfounded promises and so you know avoided like the plague be credible and so I think we have a good shot at giving a substantial increase in response let me give it a shot in term beyond that though in terms of selecting the client the first place there's some things that are really important in fact absolutely essential and a lot of these things are things that you just wouldn't think of off the top of your head when you're thinking about partnering the first thing is chemistry you've got to like the guy or gal that's running the company you've got to enjoy spending time with them you have because you're gonna be spending a lot of time and they need to like you there needs to be you know there's sometimes you just look at somebody and you want to smack them in the nose maybe they remind you of a maybe they remind you of a cop that gave them a ticket yesterday or something but or a mean uncle that you had but you know I have that effect on a lot of people and so chemistry is important you know but just in terms of personal chemistry having some things in common you can chat about beyond work is also helpful also when it comes to chemistry I think everyone on this call knows that that I have a partnering relationship with Weiss research Martin and I are polar opposites in every sense of the word I'm a high school dropout he's a Columbia PhD I'm I'm more right brain than left brain I have very heavy creative aptitude and some accounting aptitude but I lean towards the right brain Martin is almost a hundred percent left brain and extremely detail-oriented extremely detail-oriented he has flashes brilliance on the creative side but you know normal operating mode is very left brain over things things detail-oriented till hell won't have it and that that that difference in our personalities that that just vast difference golf between us personality wise has actually proven to be a huge moneymaker for us because we compliment each other and it's funny how some of the you know I see this all the time where they interview these old couples have been married for two or three hundred years and then how come it worked you know half of all the couples from America get divorced how come you guys stuck together and more often than not one of them or the other or both will say you know what we're just so different that we compliment each other we complete each other and so the most successful relationships I've had with clients have been relationships in which we completed each other on a personal level number two is desire the client's got to have a fire in his belly he's got to really want to do this because it's not going to be easy it's not going to happen overnight but he's got to want to grow the company and no I actually you think that's a given if you own a company you want to grow it but I've had a couple of experiences one where I partnered with a client and his wife didn't want to grow the company she was happy having a small family-oriented firm and so there was a constant struggle between my client's wife and I and finally at one point and I don't know where it was you know what caused it and my client simply told his wife you can stop coming into the office now because I'm doing this with Clayton and then we're gonna sell the company and then you and I can go do whatever we want to with the rest of our lives but I mean that's how committed this guy was to growing his company on the other hand I had another guy come to visit me when I lived in Sarasota the company owner and I think most people on the call would know the company and we route to dinner one night and I just completed a promotion for this guy and I'm looking for him to mail it big time and be aggressive and make me lots of royalties and at dinner he says you know I don't want to be a big company I you know I'm about the size I want to be now I'm really not interested in growth so you know there aren't people like that out there and it's good to know that before before you you know jump into bed with them another thing I look for is that the company needs to have a customer list and there it should be ideally it should be underused or poorly used or at least show opportunities you know where I could use it to better advantage and when I say customer list I mean more than just their name and their email address and their street address I mean order history what they bought when they bought it what they responded to the source where the customer originally came from you know you well we rented sharper image catalog and this guy you know came in from a promotion to that list and it's complete order history if you have that you're well on the way to being able to establish establish key metrics like customer lifetime value and to categorize customers using the RFA code concept that we discussed earlier and so forth another one is prospect universe I want to know that the company has a substantial enough list of prospects in the first place to warrant my going to work with them in other words I can grow them that way I've written in the you know in the past about and this is new with me a lot of other guys written about it but there are only just a few ways to grow a company you can get the more customers you can get each customer to order more often you can get each customer to make bigger purchases every time you are he orders and you can get a customer to stay with you longer okay so a big part of that of course is having a prospect universe is large enough so that you can do the first thing which is to grow the customer base and that can be a little tricky but you know go to the library get the standard rate data service and look up the prospective clients industry and catalog sales look at catalog sales or if it's you know newsletters look that up and and look at what all of his competitors are doing and try to get a sense for how large the number of active customers really is in that universe a few years ago it can change radically a few years ago there were probably about 10 million prospects in the investment newsletter universe today I'd be surprised that there were a million and and I again by by prospects I mean people who within the last 12 months have subscribed to an investment newsletter so you know that universe has shrunk notably okay and so you know be aware of that and be aware of how how large the universe is look at the hotline names that are listed in the SRDS the standard rate data service for this guy's customers and also for his company and the hotline names of the people who bought within the last specified period of time usually 60 days 90 days and that tells you how quickly they're adding customers to their file now so that gives you a sense of their growth potential I mentioned earlier the company needs to have or be willing to develop a product matrix that that includes products and offers at attractive prices for new customers as well as at higher prices for existing customers you can maximize revenues the company needs to have sufficient infrastructure to support a marketing push because you don't want to be hiring list brokers and you know print buyers and media buyers and doing all of that the company should have people who can handle that for you and or be willing to hire them be willing to recruit and hire them and then finally at the very least the company should have enough capital set aside to sustain operations for a reasonable period of time and again that sounds like a no-brainer but you'd be surprised I mean I had meetings last year with a company that looked really great and they had a very small cash crisis I guess most of us it would seem small for a company I think they had somebody Renegade on a $70,000 debt to the company and it almost put them out of business and it froze their marketing the company doesn't have to have a large word chest though and that's cool because if they have a customer file especially if that customer file has not been fully exploited that's money in the bank and that's money that you can extract in 30 days so all it takes is a well-crafted customer file promotion either campaign or even a single hit and you can generally pull big money out of a customer file that can finance your marketing going forward I've told the story before again I'll hold up the three on this one this is my Dan Rosenfeld story but Dan came into a company that was one of the I'd say smaller mid-size financial publishers and says tell you what you guys got 25,000 or 30,000 paying customers active right now and you know you just not promoting anything to these people after the fact you know you're making all your money on renewals but really not offering any other products these people and so you know I want to offer them a $5,000 service now these are people that spent $99 to subscribe to the newsletter and so Dan says I want to offer them a $5,000 product it'll be a trading product trade trade trade options interest rate options and so the guy goes okay so Dan and writes the promotion and they put it in the mail and about a week later they're counting $10 million in sales out of a file of less than 30,000 names in less than 30 days if you're a copywriter marketer and you walk into a company and you see a list a customer list that's underused or poorly used not fully leveraged basically a virgin list is what Dan walked into in this particular deal and you take just 10% of the revenues that's a million dollars 30 days the more importantly the company now has $9 million that is sitting on now in this case the fulfillment of the product was online so there was really no cost no fulfillment cost so that was pretty much that money so now the company had nine million dollars and it's worth just to do any darn thing it wanted to in terms of extracting new customers so that is really the goal I mean if you can find a good customer list that's been underutilized you don't have to have a lot of capital in the company just just enough to make sure they continue operations because you could you could strike gold with it with that customer list okay but these are these a lot of times are what you're just described is obviously a royalty or percentage deal and how do you trust though how do you trust that somebody's gonna actually pay you let's say that you you you make somebody a couple million dollars doing this and then what's to prevent them from you know not saying see ya you know no I didn't I didn't say that you know right anything yeah well first of all you can't trust a contract to help you because not with that anyway there are there are a lot of great things the contract can do for you but frankly any contract is only as good as the character of the counterparty signs it so what I would I like to do and you know admittedly I tend to focus in a couple of niches and these are highly incestuous niches and so I know you know all the copywriters who write in the health and investment newsletter markets and in the supplement markets and all the all the successful ones at least and I know the the major players and there are some of the major players that I wouldn't work with if you held a gun to my head because I have heard from copywriters that they've reneged or or cheated or or you know took shortcuts around paying royalties there there are ways to do that they're not transparent copywriters figured out and word gets out and before you know it that particular guy is having trouble finding top quality talent so word does get around so if you're in another if you're in a different that's not as incestuous what I would suggest is that when you were having your first discussions with the company owner that you find subtle ways to find out who he's worked with before and chances are he'll you know I'd love to have dinner with the client before I go people you know and so you know after I've kind of pre-qualified him on the phone in long distance I'll jump on a plane they'll generally agree to pick up your expenses is it about out there for some face-to-face time and I like meeting with the client over dinner the night before I go into the office and just having a nice relaxed time and talk a little bit about his history his past what he's experienced and part of that is finding out who he's worked with before then a simple matter of when you get home to ring some of those people up and talk to them about have they worked with this guy before you know what was he fair with you did he pay his bills did he pay on time was he right on the money with the royalties and those kinds of functional questions the so first of all you know established that you're talking to somebody who is trustworthy and frankly a smart paint a smart client will overpay a copywriter market a marketer that he values if anything because he understands it I know a guy who's in this situation right now he understands that if you have a dispute even your if you're right if you have a dispute with a copywriter and the copywriter goes on down the road that word gets around fast and it's gonna be hard to get good talent the other thing is I require daily promotion reports now you know if you're doing direct mail the reports don't have to be daily but I'm doing direct mail as well as daily web-based promotions and so I ask for a daily promotion report that shows me what the response rate average sale and total amount of dollars in is on every promotion that we're doing every promotion that's active and I one client that sends me those promotions four times a day he's automated the process and so I know what his cancels are what his refund requests are what how many people order today how many people cancel what our total revenues are the whole nine yards now this isn't this is not something I asked for to keep the client honest this is something I asked for because I need this data in order to suggest strategies tactics and so forth to improve his response an average sale down the road and see how people are responding to different kinds of offers to monitor our test panels and with web marketing where you where you're emailing to a file every single day or six days week as we often do it allows you to make mid-course corrections on the fly if a particular appeal or offer isn't working you don't have to wait some period of time in order to know that you know it within hours and you're able to make maybe maybe do a flight of real quick tests or do something else to change the direction of campaign to maximize response and sales so you know that's how I do it and the bottom line is the as I said before it's in the the client's best interest to make you very happy if you're producing huge I mean imagine that client rose all goes in produces 10 million dollars in a week in a promotion that ran over a week I think it took him like a week or 10 days to write it then promotion itself just lasted a week he produces 10 million dollars no imagine that client sheeping out on rose thought that this guy's just handed you nine million dollars okay and at that point if you get cheap with that with the say at a 10% royalty I think Dan got more than that but let's say at a 10% commission on sales you cheap out on that one million dollars and it's gonna cost you the next 10 million he could have produced so you know it would be nuts to do that and in my experience very few people do so okay well another thing that that I wanted to cover was you know I'm very detail oriented and and I think that there's a I want I really want to keep people to complete picture and I know that that that not everything is perfect and utopian with with this model so if there's anything else that you can think of in terms of issues or pitfalls of things that that somebody's getting into this has to watch for or problems that they may have to address that I would love to hear that well there's there are a lot of things to watch out for I've been burned by all of these at one point or another but if there's a change in ownership of the company you know put on put on your running shoes be ready to bolt it doesn't mean you have to but it means those things just generally don't work out well change in management a lot of times you'll take a small company make it big and the owner will say okay now I want to see you so I don't have to do the day-to-day stuff that's always a red flag because the new management is that also quite often doesn't work out well you may find enough versus them mentality among the client staff where they resist ideas from outside they want to run the show you threaten their position and so an advertorial adversarial adverse adversarial yeah yeah relationships arises a change in the regulatory environment or compliance regulations that a company is going to signal a change in response rates and so that gets my attention the company is experiencing legal problems not just you know trivial lawsuit or something like that but you know you find out the company is being investigated or accused of doing something grossly wrong that is another red flag to look out for you know a year before 9-11 I was sitting at the top of the World Trade Center in New York almost to the day being grilled by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission because I had accepted three assignments from a newsletter editor who turned out to be one of the one of the biggest scam artists and scumbags I never heard of but I didn't know that time I accepted the work and it ended up costing me six weeks of work at $20,000 in legal fees just to get through the deposition so you know legal problems can be a biggie and there's a lot you can do to cover yourself there with contracts to we're not gonna have time for that today by the way we're a hour and 20 minutes into this hour long call but I say we just keep going yeah I still have a few more questions I'd like to get handle okay good if you're down if you're up for it I am yeah I'm cool okay might as well get all these thoughts out right yep okay a change in market psychology is also a big red flag you know we saw that happen in the investment markets in about 2002 you know we had the tech wreck and all of that kind of you know and then we had 9-11 and anthrax in the mail and then suddenly our 10 million strong prospect universe shrunk to about 1 million and people just decided they didn't need investment newsletters anymore so you know a change in market psychology is a big sign of danger and a change in the product quality or becoming aware that product was never quite what you assumed it was in terms of quality all of those things are danger signs to look out for having said all of that the one thing that there's actually something that you could do deal with you know if the owner decides to sell many of your business okay they're gonna do it whether you like it or not if they decide that they want new management or if there's a change in the regulatory environment or if they have legal there's nothing you can do about those things the one thing you could deal with is the us versus that mentality on the client staff and that is very common and you can deal with that and the best the best way I know to deal with the kind of sniping and sometimes sabotage that will happen as staff members attempt to prove that you're no damn good and that the company doesn't need you and it will happen it does happen because people are threatened by outside experts the best way to deal with that is is to not allow it to happen quietly in the background and bring it out into the sunlight the first time it happens and every time it happens and have a little chat on the phone with the person and with the owner and you know just deal with it like an adult and it's amazing how quickly it stops when you do that so how helpful is it to be friends you know those people in those kinds of I know some people obviously they're just bullied ran or they have issues and then they're not gonna they're not gonna come around but have you have you found it valuable to make friends with people you know other than the owner other than your primary contact so that things run more you know to be like almost like a politician a little bit you know make your agenda your agenda get pushed yeah you you really need to and you know it's whenever I'm doing these deals I insist first of all that my primary contact is the company owner and the strategic issues are discussed between the two of us now in some cases there you know he'll want his general manager involved in this or he'll want another person also there and that's cool that's fine so long as the as long as the company owner is on board and that and that you guys agree on the strategy and the priority list and you know the way that you're going to address things it doesn't matter who else is on the call that's all fine and good the second thing though is that sometimes I've had it happen where the company owner and I would you know spend our time together and lay out the strategy and then he would go back and tell the staff and they'd all be upset because they weren't involved decision-making again giving some staff members the opportunity to be involved in those strategy meetings is important secondly urging the client to communicate effectively with his staff is important and third making it clear from the outside with with a company owner that you want to communicate with his staff about these things is important and when you do that is important that you not force feed them or make it seem like they're being forced that give you a good example we have a major a major push going on right now that involves seven PPC campaigns for seven analysts right with a gauntlet series including videos and free reports and all kinds of things for seven analysts and and then ultimately a sale as far as gauntlet and again a gauntlet is the strategy that you use to handle and to create bonding and hopefully also create a secondary sale in a new lead or a new customers first 30 60 days on file okay so this is as you can imagine with seven different of seven of these campaigns going being created at the same time getting the strategy right getting everyone on board there's there's a heavy web component there's a direct mail component there's a video component so you know all of the right people had to be on board with this and so I did a webinar you know I just I prepared this PowerPoint presentation and here's what Martin and I've been talking about and here's here's how we kind of see it happening and the purpose of this thing is to get your input your ideas to identify things that can't be done or you know we'll have to get additional resources to do and to just get your general opinion on it and works great you know everyone gets to contribute to the process and everyone's on board plus everyone knows which direction you're going which always helps so bringing bringing the staff into the process is extremely important I think you know in a couple times right there you just mentioned you mentioned the strategy and obviously you and I both both know that like you know the best copy in the world can a lot of times fail if there's a faulty strategy behind it whereas you know average copy a lot of times with a great strategy will make the client a lot of money so how do you ensure that you and the client are on the same page when it comes to strategy and really the execution of that strategy but it's you know I'm kind of linear in the way that I think and so you know I kind of structure it in a way that makes sense for me and the way the thing that's worked best for me over the years is that I attempt quarterly face-to-face meetings with a client I live up here in you know the Booneys in Western North Carolina and my biggest clients in Florida and I have clients in several other states I try to have face-to-face strategy meetings with those clients once a quarter sometimes it's once every five months or you know four months but fairly regularly during the year and it's usually suggested suggested the timing is often suggested by a major promotion or or a new product that we're launching or whatever but those quarterly face-to-face meetings are very important and they're important for political reasons as well as just to make sure everyone's on the same page and then secondly I have regular telephone conferences that are scheduled and and the you know so many times a week or so many times a month on a particular day at a particular time and those times are in violent they happen no matter what else there could be an earthquake you know in California and a tornado in North Carolina somehow we still have it it's absolutely essential to respond to you know new opportunities new challenges changes the market and so forth and to really stay on the same page stay in touch I've become a Skype addict lately I love Skype I have it you know running all the time he's like a little bunny rabbit he pops up every 50 minutes goes you there you there but the yeah the Skype is great because you know I have my clients all on it you know I'm writing something and I'm stuck or I have a question or have an idea that I want to you know kind of get off get off script on and I'll have to do this type of quick chat chat Skype chat to the guy and I've got my answer in a matter of seconds but tell you know I use a telephone and Skype very heavily that way email is probably the worst anytime I can avoid email when talking to a client I do it's you know and it drives me crazy when people are you know constantly using email and then and then you know the person doesn't get back to them and you know and then you say well you know what did you find out about this well I emailed it went two weeks ago well you and you haven't heard back no you see this thing on your desk that we paid a lot of money put this got all the buttons on it you know that damn phone call them rattle or cage you know we got to get done email is a problem because I think it actually extends the amount of time it takes to get something done but also because it quite often can be taken in the worst possible way and create political problems in a client's organization you might say something perfectly innocently and have somebody read it and feel as a personal attack on them simply because you copied a couple of people that they're sensitive to so now email I try to avoid less I'm trying to create a permanent record of a particular communication and I try to use more spontaneous ways of communicating instead okay another thing another thing that I've observed and I'm sure people on our list that as well is that a lot of times there are companies with a great product a product that you know a market the market is hot the product is really good and they're in need of marketing but at the same time also their company is just a total mess maybe they have bad management my employees just their level of organization and in terms of systems and everything that's going on is is way off do you try to fix are there are there situations where you try to fix that go in and fix all that other stuff on top of the marketing are you do you just know I count it I've done it but you know it just depends you know if the company's a lost cause you know I wouldn't have accepted them in the first place because remember the beginning of the process for me is that face-to-face meeting and so I would have determined early on that you know the company was a basket case and was itself an obstacle to creating a success but sometimes the product is just so good you know or the and the opportunity seems so huge maybe it's a huge prospect universe or they got a big list or you know there's some other reason why you really really want to do this but the company is a basket case and they really need major help on the on every level right I dive in with both feet I mean I if the money is there if the potential for growth is there and so strong that I just can't resist temptation you know what can I do I'm gonna have to do it but I I I would never begin by going in and trying to turn the world upside down I'd much rather have it happen kind of naturally as relationship deepness because you know it's like a car you know that you know a car in a particular old rattle trap might do just great at 35 miles an hour just kind of plugging along but the minute you put your foot in the carburetor and try to get some real performance out of that thing and take it up to 80 90 miles an hour all of a sudden you know the tires are shaking off the rims and the doors are rattling and the and the engine's coughing and and you know now you're starting because you're putting the machine under stress the flaws in the machine are starting to make themselves evident and once you get involved with a company and you begin exploding that company's revenues profits increasing the number of promotions going out doing those things those cracks become glaringly obvious not just to you you may have spotted them before but now the company owner is spotting them in self and so instead of feeling asked to defend his staff or his organization his organizational structure or his internal procedures or his infrastructure in terms of it or our you know software whatever instead of feeling like you know you're attacking and criticizing and he's defending now he's saying holy cow man we we got to fix this is costing us money so again the watch word is let it happen naturally you know go in do your job right great sales copy come up with great strategies and then as the company begins accelerating in its growth in its growth cycle then then these things will become obvious and the owner is going to be coming to you and saying how do we fix this you know there were 27 typos in that last email how do I make sure that doesn't happen or you know they sent it to the wrong file segment or you know you know we got a new control in January now it's March and we're still not out with a rollout you know what procedures do we need in place or what people do we need in place to be able to increase the number of mailings we do per year so those kinds of things can happen naturally the decent of the fact that the company is your your client's baby and although he's frustrated with the growth of the company you know thinks you know he's got the best product in the world the world should be beating a path to the store the fact of the matter is he built that company he selected those people and and you know one of the things I hear most often and drives me crazy just because everyone says it is that you know we're a family you know it's not a company we're a little family you know we're all involved in each other's lives and blah blah blah we're all best friends yeah right you know the minute you have to cut back staff and one of your best friends has to be laid off I bet you it'll be 20 years before you ever hear from them again you know they're not your best friends are not your family they're your employees but there's a sense that in small companies that oh we're all just a little family and the the so you know when you go in and you start telling this guy that is family is a bunch of incompetent miscreants and you're not going to get a warm reception so just go ahead and let it happen naturally I see yeah you know it seems like the marketing all the marketing stuff and the corporate itself is is already a lot of work potentially you know there's there seems like there's really but no limit potentially you know you keep going and going going and that's another concern I have is as the work the work level goes and the workload grows I think even beyond if it's exceeding and they're making more money that want to grow and grow how do you manage all that work or do you do you begin to outsource some of it that that you know that yeah better suited for somebody else to be doing yeah again it's it's it's pretty natural if you just let it happen you start out by doing what you can do and then you'll get to it and then at first you know there'll be plenty to do you'll have to prioritize the work and you work your way through it you'll start to see sales taking off like crazy and then it will get to the point where there's just too much for one person to do and I had that happen with a client you know it was with Weiss in the early 2000s and that's when I uh that's how I entered a mentoring uh Paris Lampropolis, Carleen Angway Cole and Ken Komey and uh Bob Hutchinson and Brad Peterson I basically just said hey guys come on I don't have time to write all of these direct mail packages and so I want you guys to help me and not a one of them had written the long copy magalock not one of them and so you know I showed them how to write them I led them through a copy chief them and then I I really relied on them heavily now I owned kind of the client's back end okay we're at least part of it not the whole back end up part of it back then and um uh so you know for you know I made money a couple of ways I took I took half of the advance and I took half of the royalty none of these writers were to level where they could earn $50 a thousand and a $25,000 advance uh they were all making just about half that so it was a win-win I paid them their regular fees right I was able to accept uh new customer acquisition um promotions that otherwise I would have had to decline or just never would have gotten done um they and so that was free money to me basically uh and then they came in and by creating these promotions and some of them you know were just phenomenally successful um Bob Hutchinson and I created the supernet package which melt for almost three years uh and uh you know all of them all of them did great work and uh but I was I had an overwrite on the back end so every time an existing customer bought something I got a piece of that so I was making money again because they were growing our file and uh as a file grew larger our sales to existing customers grew larger so that worked great and more recently um um I had a situation where um I didn't want to handle a front end at all I I just wanted to focus on the back end because that's where the money was but we needed to grow the front end so I just I had the client call um another friend of mine Eric Patel and uh Eric is like one of the best copywriters Rodale ever had he's written huge controls for Phillips uh many other top mailers and brought Eric in and Eric wrote the front end and created thousands of new customers and then I made a bunch of money selling them stuff so um today I've got uh four full-time copywriters on staff and uh we're we're following the agoramodel here each one of them gets a good basic income that's to you know keep body and soul together but not to get rich on and uh then each one receives 10% of the money that they make me and so uh on just one client each one of them has a potential you know to make high six figures or even a million dollars a year in royalties so I decided to go that direction I was ready for it you know actually establishing an agency um but you don't have to go that way you can allow that stuff to be done by other freelancers and and take a commission or a piece of the action for introducing them to the client yeah that I understand I mean makes sense I'm starting to get that cool thanks for doing this Clayton um but it was a good call and and uh you did answer a lot of my um my question yeah it really helps it really helps me think my way through it and you know because a lot of this I've just kind of done instinctively intuitively over the years um the fact that it's worked out like gangbusters for us and and for our clients uh is uh is fortuitous and but but I I wish I could say you know 28 years ago I sat down and I came up with this great new way that's so much better I really fell into it and the stuff I'll be sharing are are more real lessons from actually doing it and uh paint and I want to paint the picture for you of what it feels like to have a client who's so in love with you that he refuses to write checks he insists on wiring your royalties into your account directly on the 15th of every month and so when you get your bank statement you open it up and look at it oh we got 650,000 this month oh we got 300,000 this month you know and and that you know that's not an exaggeration and and I was actually there once when he told his account this one client told his account to send the money and he was giggling on the phone yes and Clayton they have a million dollars this month that's his royalty and he was laughing because he made 10 times that so that's great Clayton takes a lot I appreciate it I hope everybody got a lot out of it cool thank you Tony all right take it easy take care this ends module one talk about some great stuff but don't stop yet because we're just getting started we've barely scratched the surface of the make piece business system and there's plenty more still to come in the next module Clayton's gonna really dive into the details and reveal just how you can profit from his partnership model whether you're a copywriter consultant or business owner so what are we waiting for
